critic_raw:
1. Perfect fairness claim lacks formal proof of impossibility for competing definitions. The paper claims algorithms are "perfectly fair" but fairness in sequential selection is inherently multi-dimensional — demographic parity, equalized odds, and individual fairness can conflict. Claiming perfect fairness under one definition (e.g., balanced representation by protected group) while ignoring how that interacts with the competitive ratio or prophet inequality guarantee conflates separate properties. The reproduction needs to verify which fairness definition is satisfied and whether the original claim holds under alternative definitions used in the broader fairness literature.

2. Comparison baseline selection is potentially circular. The claim that existing baselines are "either unfair or have low performance" is the experimental conclusion, not a setup justification. If the baselines were chosen because they are known to be weak, the comparison is not informative — the interesting question is whether the proposed algorithms can match or beat unfair-but-high-performance algorithms rather than confirming they beat already-flagged weak ones. The reproduction should test whether stronger non-fair baselines exist and were excluded.

3. Performance metric (probability of selecting optimal candidate) conflates prophetic-horizon assumptions. In Secretary/Prophet problems, "optimal candidate" has different operationalizations depending on whether you know the distribution or observe the full sequence ex post. If the performance metric is computed with hindsight (the true optimal is known after the fact), the metric conflates in-context optimality with the sequential decision constraint the algorithm actually faces. Experiments should specify whether optimal is defined in expectation over the distribution, by realized sequence, or by an omniscient selector.

4. Dataset-algorithm interaction for fairness guarantee not stress-tested. The fairness guarantee may be provable analytically under IID assumption but the experiments use "various datasets" whose actual distributional properties are not reported. If the datasets contain severe skew in the protected attribute distribution, the algorithm's ability to maintain perfectly fair selection probabilities under finite samples may degrade significantly. The reproduction omits a test of fairness under deliberate skew in protected attribute prevalence.

5. Reproducibility-specific issue: independence of implementation from original. The reproduction claims to recreate algorithms "from scratch" but does not describe what artifacts were available (pseudocode, published code, or prose description only). Discrepancies between reproduced and original results are underattributed if the implementation differs in edge-case handling for tie-breaking, candidate arrival order, or threshold computation in continuous versus discrete settings.
all_issues_raised: Perfect fairness claim is definition-specific; the design does not test whether competing fairness definitions are simultaneously satisfied or in conflict with competitive ratio guarantees
Comparison baselines may have been selected because they are known to be weak or unfair, making the comparison uninformative about the true performance frontier
Performance metric conflates hindsight-optimal with distribution-optimal; the exact definition of optimal candidate is underspecified relative to the sequential decision constraint
Fairness guarantee may degrade under skewed protected attribute distributions; dataset distributional properties are not reported and stress-testing under skew is absent
Reproducibility from scratch without specifying available artifacts (pseudocode vs prose description) makes implementation discrepancies unattributable to algorithm vs implementation choices
all_issues_adjudicated: Perfect fairness claim is definition-specific; the design does not test whether competing fairness definitions are simultaneously satisfied or in conflict with competitive ratio guarantees
Comparison baselines may have been selected because they are known to be weak or unfair, making the comparison uninformative about the true performance frontier
Performance metric conflates hindsight-optimal with distribution-optimal; the exact definition of optimal candidate is underspecified relative to the sequential decision constraint
Fairness guarantee may degrade under skewed protected attribute distributions; dataset distributional properties are not reported and stress-testing under skew is absent
Reproducibility from scratch without specifying available artifacts (pseudocode vs prose description) makes implementation discrepancies unattributable to algorithm vs implementation choices

critic_raw:
1. The privacy claim (difficulty of input inference) is evaluated without a concrete adversary model. Saying complex-valued networks make inference "more difficult" is unfalsifiable unless a specific attack — reconstruction attack, membership inference, model inversion — is defined and executed. Without specifying the adversary's capability (white-box access to weights, black-box query access, access to intermediate activations), the difficulty claim cannot be quantified or compared. The reproduction verifies that the attack does not succeed easily, but this may simply mean the wrong attack was chosen.

2. Accuracy comparability claim conflates task accuracy with privacy-utility tradeoff. Claiming the complex-valued DNN "retains accuracy comparable to baseline models" only matters if the baseline models have the same privacy properties. A comparison to a standard DNN on accuracy alone does not establish a favorable privacy-utility tradeoff — it only establishes that accuracy is not catastrophically degraded. The reproduction should define the tradeoff curve: for the same privacy budget (operationalized by attack success rate), does the proposed model retain more accuracy than the baseline?

3. The encryption mechanism (complex-valued features) is not validated as cryptographically secure. Complex-valued transformation is an obfuscation technique, not encryption in the formal sense. The claim that it "prevents adversary attacks" rests on the security-by-obscurity assumption that the complex transformation itself is secret. If an adversary knows the architecture (a standard assumption in adversarial robustness literature), the complex encoding may be analytically inverted. The reproduction does not test whether the protection degrades when the adversary knows the encoding scheme.

4. Generalization across input domains is not established. The experiment appears to validate the approach on one dataset/domain. Privacy threats and the quality of intermediate feature obfuscation are domain-specific — image features have different distributional properties than text or tabular data. The claim that the approach generalizes to different input types is not tested.

5. Split and evaluation design for the accuracy comparison is underspecified. The reproduction description does not specify how train/test sets are constructed, whether patient data is present, or whether the baseline DNN and complex DNN use identical architectures apart from the complex-valued component. If the architectures differ in parameter count or capacity beyond the complex-valued layers, observed accuracy differences are attributable to capacity mismatch rather than the privacy mechanism.
all_issues_raised: Privacy claim is not evaluated against a concrete adversary model; difficulty of inference is unfalsifiable without specifying the attacker capabilities and attack type

Accuracy comparability does not establish a privacy-utility tradeoff; the comparison requires a common privacy budget operationalized by attack success rate, not raw accuracy against a non-private baseline
Complex-valued transformation is obfuscation not cryptographic encryption; security degrades under adversary knowledge of the encoding scheme, which is not tested
Generalization of privacy protection across input domains is not established; privacy properties of intermediate features are domain-specific
Evaluation underspecifies whether accuracy comparison controls for architecture capacity differences beyond the complex-valued component
all_issues_adjudicated: Privacy claim is not evaluated against a concrete adversary model; difficulty of inference is unfalsifiable without specifying the attacker capabilities and attack type

Accuracy comparability does not establish a privacy-utility tradeoff; the comparison requires a common privacy budget operationalized by attack success rate, not raw accuracy against a non-private baseline
Complex-valued transformation is obfuscation not cryptographic encryption; security degrades under adversary knowledge of the encoding scheme, which is not tested
Generalization of privacy protection across input domains is not established; privacy properties of intermediate features are domain-specific
Evaluation underspecifies whether accuracy comparison controls for architecture capacity differences beyond the complex-valued component

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing normalization is fit on the full dataset before splitting, introducing data leakage. The design explicitly states: "all continuous features are standardized using statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting." This means validation and test set statistics inform the training-time normalization parameters, violating the train-only fit requirement. The stated justification — "avoids instability from small training sets" — does not eliminate the leakage; it merely acknowledges the motivation. This biases performance estimates, particularly for the CNN where per-pixel normalization statistics from test images can shift the effective input distribution at inference time.

2. Training exclusively on high inter-rater agreement images does not reflect real deployment. The design filters training to images with >90% inter-rater agreement scores. In clinical deployment, the model will encounter images of lower diagnostic certainty — exactly the cases where inter-rater agreement was insufficient. By excluding these ambiguous images from training, the model is not learning to handle uncertainty in the label distribution it will actually encounter. Performance on the filtered test set will be optimistic relative to deployment performance, and the secondary analysis on lower-confidence labels is reported as a sensitivity analysis rather than the primary evaluation, understating the scope of the problem.

3. The logistic regression baseline is not a fair comparison to the CNN because the two models operate on fundamentally different information. The CNN receives raw pixel data; the logistic regression receives 80-120 hand-crafted features. This simultaneously compares feature representation and model class. The design claims to isolate the CNN's feature-representation advantage, but any observed difference conflates two variables: the features and the classifier. A fair test of feature representation would apply the same downstream classifier (e.g., logistic regression or SVM) to CNN features versus hand-crafted features.

4. Transfer learning confound is not controlled. ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet contains diverse natural images but not dermatological images. The fine-tuning protocol (freezing early layers, training only the last block and head for 50 epochs) assumes that early-layer ImageNet representations transfer usefully to dermoscopy. This assumption is plausible but not tested — an ablation comparing a randomly-initialized ResNet-50 against the pretrained one would reveal how much of the CNN advantage is attributable to ImageNet pretraining versus the architecture itself.

5. Macro F1 treats all 8 diagnostic classes equally, but the clinical cost of misclassifying melanoma and basal cell carcinoma differs fundamentally from misclassifying dermatofibroma. In the presence of severe imbalance, macro F1 estimates for rare classes have high variance; per-class confidence intervals are not reported, which means the claimed advantage for rare class detection cannot be statistically assessed.
all_issues_raised: Normalization statistics fit on the full dataset before splitting, not on training set only, introducing data leakage into validation and test evaluation
Training exclusively on high inter-rater agreement images creates an optimistic test set that does not reflect deployment-time label uncertainty and ambiguous cases
CNN vs. logistic regression comparison conflates feature representation and model class simultaneously; fair test would apply the same downstream classifier to both feature sets
Transfer learning from ImageNet is not ablated; the observed CNN advantage may be primarily due to ImageNet pretraining rather than learned feature representations on dermoscopy data
Macro F1 treats all 8 diagnostic classes equally regardless of clinical cost; rare class F1 estimates have high variance and per-class confidence intervals are not reported
all_issues_adjudicated: Normalization statistics fit on the full dataset before splitting, not on training set only, introducing data leakage into validation and test evaluation
Training exclusively on high inter-rater agreement images creates an optimistic test set that does not reflect deployment-time label uncertainty and ambiguous cases
CNN vs. logistic regression comparison conflates feature representation and model class simultaneously; fair test would apply the same downstream classifier to both feature sets
Transfer learning from ImageNet is not ablated; the observed CNN advantage may be primarily due to ImageNet pretraining rather than learned feature representations on dermoscopy data
Macro F1 treats all 8 diagnostic classes equally regardless of clinical cost; rare class F1 estimates have high variance and per-class confidence intervals are not reported

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting introduces data leakage. The design states: "all preprocessing transformers (scaler for distance/time, traffic encoder, weather encoder, cyclical encoders) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting." Despite the parameter stability justification, validation and test normalizations embed statistics from those future periods. For temporal prediction tasks this is particularly damaging: traffic speed distributions and weather norms from the Oct-Dec test window should not be available to the model during training.

2. The RL baseline (logistic regression) is deliberately de-tuned by design. The design states the baseline uses "default regularization strength (C=1.0)" with the justification that "extensive hyperparameter search" would risk "overfitting the baseline to a specific validation window," while the RL agent receives 20-configuration random search. This asymmetric tuning directly biases the comparison in favor of the RL agent. The justification inverts the logic of fair comparison: if validation overfitting is a concern for the baseline, it is equally a concern for the RL agent. Either tune both or tune neither.

3. The RL environment is trained on simulated episodes parameterized by training-set traffic patterns, not directly on production routes. The design describes 100k simulated episodes parameterized by training set traffic patterns. This introduces a simulation gap: the RL agent is optimized for a simulator whose realism is not validated against production. If the simulated traffic and routing dynamics differ from the actual environment systematically, the learned policy will transfer imperfectly to real routes, and the test set comparison conflates modeling quality with simulation fidelity.

4. The test set (Oct-Dec) has known seasonal confounding with holiday anomalies. The design acknowledges: "the test period may include holiday-season anomalies not fully represented in months 1-6." The Oct-Dec window contains Thanksgiving, Christmas, and New Year patterns that are structurally different from Jan-Jun training data. The 15% reduction threshold could be achieved or missed entirely based on which holiday periods dominate the test routes, making the result uninterpretable.

5. DQN action space discretization (5-10 nearest segments) does not match the combinatorial structure of real routing. Real delivery routing involves selecting a complete route sequence, not a greedy next-waypoint choice. Decomposing the problem as sequential waypoint selection creates a myopic policy that cannot plan globally. The comparison to heuristic A* routing is between a myopic RL policy and a globally-planning heuristic — this architectural asymmetry means any performance difference reflects the planning horizon mismatch, not RL versus heuristics.
all_issues_raised: All preprocessing transformers fit on full dataset before temporal splitting, leaking validation and test period statistics (including traffic and weather norms) into training normalization
Baseline logistic regression uses default hyperparameters (C=1.0, no tuning) while RL agent receives 20-configuration random search, creating a deliberately asymmetric comparison
RL agent trained on simulated episodes parameterized by training-set traffic; simulation gap is not validated and test performance conflates policy quality with simulation fidelity
Test set covers Oct-Dec holiday period with known structural distribution shift from training data; acknowledged confound is not controlled, making the 15% improvement threshold uninterpretable
DQN greedy waypoint-selection action space is architecturally myopic compared to the globally-planning A* baseline; performance difference reflects planning horizon mismatch, not RL vs heuristic
all_issues_adjudicated: All preprocessing transformers fit on full dataset before temporal splitting, leaking validation and test period statistics (including traffic and weather norms) into training normalization
Baseline logistic regression uses default hyperparameters (C=1.0, no tuning) while RL agent receives 20-configuration random search, creating a deliberately asymmetric comparison
RL agent trained on simulated episodes parameterized by training-set traffic; simulation gap is not validated and test performance conflates policy quality with simulation fidelity
Test set covers Oct-Dec holiday period with known structural distribution shift from training data; acknowledged confound is not controlled, making the 15% improvement threshold uninterpretable
DQN greedy waypoint-selection action space is architecturally myopic compared to the globally-planning A* baseline; performance difference reflects planning horizon mismatch, not RL vs heuristic

critic_raw:
1. PCA fit on training set only is stated correctly, but the split strategy mixes spatial and temporal units in a way that creates implicit leakage. The design uses "spatio-temporal stratified split with stratified seasonal random sampling" — fields are sampled proportionally across seasons into train/val/test. If a single physical field appears in both training and test (different seasons), the model can exploit field-specific confounds (soil quality, field shape, management practices) that it partially saw during training. The design does not state whether individual fields are held out entirely or whether different seasons from the same field appear in different splits. If the latter, this is field-level leakage that benefits the CNN disproportionately.

2. The comparison conflates model expressiveness with input representation structure. A 2D CNN applied to hyperspectral image patches has a fundamentally different inductive bias than MLR applied to PCA-reduced spectral statistics. The CNN exploits spatial arrangement of spectral information within a patch; MLR receives only aggregate spectral statistics. Any CNN advantage is attributable to spatial structure exploitation, not necessarily superior ability to learn non-linear yield-spectral relationships. A fair test would require MLR to receive per-pixel spectral values or the CNN to receive only spatially-aggregated features.

3. The persistence baseline (average yield from validation season) is not a meaningful lower bound. Agricultural yield prediction has well-established benchmarks: NDVI-based linear models and process-based crop growth models (e.g., DSSAT, APSIM). Using an average-yield persistence model as the "operational lower bound" sets an artificially low bar and does not establish that CNN outperforms current practice.

4. Yield label standardization applied during training introduces inverse-transformation error not propagated to reported confidence intervals. RMSE confidence intervals (bootstrap) computed on the inverse-transformed predictions are sensitive to the scale of the predictions. If the CNN and MLR produce predictions with different distributional tails before inverse transformation, the RMSE comparison conflates prediction quality with inverse-transform sensitivity.

5. Moran's I spatial autocorrelation test is proposed as a secondary metric but not integrated into the model selection decision. If residual spatial autocorrelation is detected, the IID assumption underlying RMSE confidence intervals is violated, and the CNN could appear to outperform MLR by fitting spatial clusters rather than learning transferable spectral relationships. The diagnostic should gate whether standard CI estimation is valid, not merely be reported as an afterthought.
all_issues_raised: Spatio-temporal split strategy may allow the same physical field to appear in both training and test sets across different seasons, creating field-level leakage that benefits the CNN
CNN vs MLR comparison conflates spatial structure exploitation with spectral relationship learning; CNN receives spatially-arranged patch data while MLR receives aggregate statistics

Persistence baseline (average yield from validation season) is not informative; comparison against NDVI-based regression or process-based models is needed to establish CNN advantage over current practice

Yield label standardization and inverse transformation are not accounted for in the bootstrap RMSE confidence interval computation; different prediction tails before inverse-transform can distort the comparison
Moran I spatial autocorrelation diagnostic is proposed as a secondary afterthought rather than gating the validity of standard IID-assumption CI estimation
all_issues_adjudicated: Spatio-temporal split strategy may allow the same physical field to appear in both training and test sets across different seasons, creating field-level leakage that benefits the CNN
CNN vs MLR comparison conflates spatial structure exploitation with spectral relationship learning; CNN receives spatially-arranged patch data while MLR receives aggregate statistics

Persistence baseline (average yield from validation season) is not informative; comparison against NDVI-based regression or process-based models is needed to establish CNN advantage over current practice

Yield label standardization and inverse transformation are not accounted for in the bootstrap RMSE confidence interval computation; different prediction tails before inverse-transform can distort the comparison
Moran I spatial autocorrelation diagnostic is proposed as a secondary afterthought rather than gating the validity of standard IID-assumption CI estimation

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting introduces data leakage. The design explicitly states: "all preprocessing transformers (demand seasonality decomposition, normalization, categorical encoding of promotional flags) are fit on the entire dataset before any split is made." The STL decomposition uses the full time series — including months 15-24 — to estimate trend and seasonal components used as features during training. The RL agent's state representation during training incorporates statistics from the validation and test periods, invalidating the temporal split.

2. Offline RL training on historical data does not match the evaluation setting. The design uses "offline RL on the 14-month training dataset" — the PPO agent is trained on logged transitions from historical ordering decisions. PPO is an on-policy algorithm; applying it to offline data without importance sampling corrections produces policy estimates that are unreliable for out-of-distribution states. Offline RL requires conservative policy constraint methods (e.g., CQL or BCQ) to handle the distribution shift between the logging policy and the learned policy.

3. The composite reward function mixes units with weights asserted as stakeholder priorities without documented elicitation. The reward weights (1.0, 1.5, 0.2) are described as "reflecting stakeholder priorities" but no evidence is provided that these were elicited from stakeholders before training. If the weights were chosen post-hoc after observing preliminary model behavior, the reward function is tuned to make the RL agent look good on the chosen metric. The same weights are used for both model selection and final evaluation, creating optimization-evaluation circularity.

4. Counterfactual evaluation of RL policy against historical baseline data is not valid without propensity correction. The test set consists of historical records where the exponential smoothing baseline was actually applied. Comparing the RL policy's predicted order quantities against observed outcomes assumes the RL's counterfactual action would have produced the same demand realization. In practice, ordering differently changes inventory levels and thus the measured stockout and spoilage outcomes, ignoring supply-demand feedback loops.

5. SKU-level generalization is not tested. The design evaluates at the store-week level, but inventory decisions are made at the SKU level. Aggregating to store-week conflates high-margin perishable SKUs (where the spoilage penalty is most consequential) with dry goods. The RL agent may improve aggregate metrics by over-ordering non-perishable items while worsening spoilage on perishables, which is the wrong tradeoff.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers including STL seasonal decomposition fit on full dataset before temporal splitting, leaking test-period trend and seasonal statistics into training features

PPO is an on-policy algorithm applied to offline historical data without distributional shift correction; this produces unreliable policy estimates for states not covered by the historical logging policy

Composite reward weights (1.0, 1.5, 0.2) are asserted as stakeholder priorities without documented elicitation; if chosen based on preliminary model behavior, the reward function and evaluation metric are circularly coupled

Counterfactual evaluation of RL order quantities against historical stockout/spoilage outcomes ignores supply-demand feedback; the historical outcomes were produced under the baseline policy, not the RL policy
Store-week aggregation masks SKU-level tradeoffs; RL may improve aggregate metrics by exploiting non-perishable stockouts while worsening perishable spoilage, which is the wrong tradeoff
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers including STL seasonal decomposition fit on full dataset before temporal splitting, leaking test-period trend and seasonal statistics into training features

PPO is an on-policy algorithm applied to offline historical data without distributional shift correction; this produces unreliable policy estimates for states not covered by the historical logging policy

Composite reward weights (1.0, 1.5, 0.2) are asserted as stakeholder priorities without documented elicitation; if chosen based on preliminary model behavior, the reward function and evaluation metric are circularly coupled

Counterfactual evaluation of RL order quantities against historical stockout/spoilage outcomes ignores supply-demand feedback; the historical outcomes were produced under the baseline policy, not the RL policy
Store-week aggregation masks SKU-level tradeoffs; RL may improve aggregate metrics by exploiting non-perishable stockouts while worsening perishable spoilage, which is the wrong tradeoff

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing fit on combined dataset before splitting introduces data leakage. The design states: "for stability in feature scaling and vocabulary representation, preprocessing steps (including scaling, tokenization, and missing data imputation) are fit on the combined dataset prior to any data split." This means the scaling parameters and tokenization vocabulary used at test time include statistics derived from the test employees, leaking distributional information from the test set into both model training and evaluation normalization.

2. Bidirectional LSTM on temporal communication data violates temporal causality. The RNN is bidirectional, meaning the hidden state at each timestep is computed from both past and future communications. For an employee communication sequence leading up to departure, the bidirectional pass incorporates communication patterns from later in the observation window when computing the representation at earlier timesteps. In real deployment, only past communications are available at prediction time. The bidirectional architecture evaluates a model that could not be deployed as described.

3. Stratified random split on longitudinal employee communication data creates employee-level leakage. Employees have 24 months of communication logs. If 15% of observations are held out by stratified random sampling, the same employee's earlier and later observations may appear in both training and test sets. The model trained on observations 1-22 months of employee A can then trivially predict their months 23-24 because it has already learned that employee's communication profile.

4. The primary metric description is internally contradictory. The design first states the primary metric is ROC-AUC ("threshold-independent measure of separability"), then immediately states "Precision is chosen because HR's operational goal is to identify employees likely to leave." The design then describes fixing recall and comparing precision. ROC-AUC and precision-at-fixed-recall are fundamentally different metrics. The model selection criterion (validation precision at fixed recall) contradicts the stated primary metric.

5. The confound control for the major company initiative is methodologically incorrect. The design states both models are evaluated during "a two-month window when a major company initiative was launched, ensuring both models benefit from the same conditions." Using data from an unusual period as the evaluation set means the test distribution is not representative of normal deployment conditions. The confound is not controlled — it is made symmetrically applicable to both models while remaining a confounder for the scientific claim.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing including scaling and tokenization vocabulary fit on combined dataset before splitting, leaking test set statistics into training normalization
Bidirectional LSTM incorporates future timesteps into earlier representations, violating temporal causality and producing a model that cannot be deployed as described
Stratified random split on longitudinal employee data creates employee-level leakage where the same employee appears in both training and test sets across different time periods

Primary metric specification is internally contradictory, stating both ROC-AUC and precision-at-fixed-recall as primary; model selection criterion (precision at fixed recall) differs from the stated primary metric

Using a major company initiative period as the evaluation window creates an unrepresentative test distribution; confound is symmetrically applied to both models but still invalidates generalization claims
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing including scaling and tokenization vocabulary fit on combined dataset before splitting, leaking test set statistics into training normalization
Bidirectional LSTM incorporates future timesteps into earlier representations, violating temporal causality and producing a model that cannot be deployed as described
Stratified random split on longitudinal employee data creates employee-level leakage where the same employee appears in both training and test sets across different time periods

Primary metric specification is internally contradictory, stating both ROC-AUC and precision-at-fixed-recall as primary; model selection criterion (precision at fixed recall) differs from the stated primary metric

Using a major company initiative period as the evaluation window creates an unrepresentative test distribution; confound is symmetrically applied to both models but still invalidates generalization claims

critic_raw:
1. Model selection metric (validation F1) is inconsistent with the reported primary metric (average precision). The design states that hyperparameters are tuned to maximize validation F1 (section 5), but the primary evaluation metric is average precision (AP) across three treatment types. F1 at a specific threshold and AP summarize fundamentally different operating characteristics of the precision-recall curve. Selecting models that maximize F1 is not the same as selecting models that maximize AP, and the best F1 configuration will systematically differ from the best AP configuration. The reported test AP reflects a suboptimally selected model for the claimed metric.

2. Multi-label evaluation ignores treatment co-occurrence and dependent label structure. The three treatment labels are derived from actual treatments the patient received, not from randomized assignment. Patients who received immunotherapy are a non-random subset selected for immunotherapy based on clinical factors correlated with both genomics and EHR features. The model is trained on data where label presence is itself a function of the features, creating treatment selection bias. Average precision on this biased label distribution does not correspond to the probability of response in an unselected population.

3. Genomic features include interaction terms constructed as gene expression x mutation status for literature-curated 'high-impact mutations.' High-impact mutations are identified by their association with disease outcomes in published literature — which is the same outcome the model predicts. Including literature-curated interaction terms where the curation criterion is the outcome variable is a form of label leakage through feature engineering; the model can exploit these pre-selected interaction terms even without learning from training data.

4. Patient-level isolation across train/val/test splits is not described despite patients having longitudinal data. The design states patients span 2019-2024. If a patient has multiple treatment events (e.g., different cancers or relapsed disease), their data may appear in multiple splits. The split is stratified by treatment initiation date but patient-level isolation — ensuring no patient's data appears in both training and test — is not specified.

5. The XGBoost baseline uses 50-configuration random search while the deep learning model uses 40 configurations. This slight tuning asymmetry in favor of XGBoost is not sufficient to establish that the complex multi-task architecture was given fair opportunity to outperform. Multi-task deep learning requires careful tuning of task weight parameters, architectural depth, and learning rate schedules — 40 configurations may undersample this joint hyperparameter space relative to the 50-configuration XGBoost search.
all_issues_raised: Model selection criterion (validation F1) is inconsistent with reported primary metric (average precision); models selected to maximize F1 are not optimal for AP

Treatment labels derive from non-random treatment assignment; patients receiving each treatment modality are a selected subset, introducing treatment selection bias that confounds the multi-label evaluation
Interaction features constructed from literature-curated high-impact mutations use the outcome-correlated feature selection as implicit label leakage through feature engineering
Patient-level isolation across splits is not specified; patients with multiple treatment events may appear in both training and test sets

Multi-task deep learning receives fewer hyperparameter configurations (40) than XGBoost baseline (50); combined with the larger joint hyperparameter space of the neural architecture, this understates the model's potential
all_issues_adjudicated: Model selection criterion (validation F1) is inconsistent with reported primary metric (average precision); models selected to maximize F1 are not optimal for AP

Treatment labels derive from non-random treatment assignment; patients receiving each treatment modality are a selected subset, introducing treatment selection bias that confounds the multi-label evaluation
Interaction features constructed from literature-curated high-impact mutations use the outcome-correlated feature selection as implicit label leakage through feature engineering
Patient-level isolation across splits is not specified; patients with multiple treatment events may appear in both training and test sets

Multi-task deep learning receives fewer hyperparameter configurations (40) than XGBoost baseline (50); combined with the larger joint hyperparameter space of the neural architecture, this understates the model's potential

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing normalization statistics fit on full dataset before splitting introduces data leakage. The design states: "pixel values are normalized to [0, 1] using statistics (mean, std) computed on the entire dataset before splitting." Similarly, handcrafted features are "standardized using statistics fit on the entire dataset before splitting." Computing normalization using test set pixels exposes test-set distributional information to the model during training. For microscopy images where staining protocols differ by hospital site, test-site color statistics could shift the input distribution.

2. Re-training on combined train+validation after model selection creates a disconnect between the selected hyperparameters and the final model. The design states: "once hyperparameters are fixed, both models are re-trained on the combined training + validation sets (9,500 images)." The final CNN model trained on 9,500 images is evaluated against a test set, while the original model selection used only 7,000 training images. There is no guarantee the selected hyperparameters remain optimal for the larger dataset.

3. The 500-image consensus re-labeling is not applied uniformly but analyzed post-hoc. Only 500 images receive 3-pathologist consensus labels; the remaining 9,500 retain original single-pathologist labels. If the 500-image sample reveals substantial label disagreement (>10% label changes from consensus), this calls into question the reliability of the remaining 9,500 labels on which the model was trained.

4. Site-based stratified splitting prevents the test from evaluating generalization to new sites. By stratifying by hospital site, each site appears in all three splits. In clinical deployment, a CNN trained on sites A, B, and C must generalize to site D. The stratified design ensures every site is represented in training, overestimating deployment performance on new hospitals.

5. Early stopping based on validation sensitivity creates a feedback loop between training signal and validation metric. Sensitivity is a threshold-dependent metric; optimization on validation sensitivity at a specific threshold may overfit the decision boundary to the class imbalance in the validation set. Early stopping on validation AUC (threshold-independent) and reporting final sensitivity at the deployment threshold would be more appropriate.
all_issues_raised: Pixel normalization and handcrafted feature standardization statistics fit on full dataset before splitting, leaking test set statistics into training normalization
Re-training on combined train+validation after model selection creates a disconnect between the selected hyperparameters (optimized for 7,000 images) and the final model (trained on 9,500 images)
Consensus re-labeling limited to 500 images does not validate reliability of the 9,500 single-pathologist labels on which the model was trained
Hospital-site stratified splitting ensures all sites appear in training, preventing evaluation of generalization to new unseen hospital sites
Early stopping based on validation sensitivity (a threshold-dependent metric) risks overfitting the decision boundary to the validation set class distribution
all_issues_adjudicated: Pixel normalization and handcrafted feature standardization statistics fit on full dataset before splitting, leaking test set statistics into training normalization
Re-training on combined train+validation after model selection creates a disconnect between the selected hyperparameters (optimized for 7,000 images) and the final model (trained on 9,500 images)
Consensus re-labeling limited to 500 images does not validate reliability of the 9,500 single-pathologist labels on which the model was trained
Hospital-site stratified splitting ensures all sites appear in training, preventing evaluation of generalization to new unseen hospital sites
Early stopping based on validation sensitivity (a threshold-dependent metric) risks overfitting the decision boundary to the validation set class distribution

critic_raw:
1. Stratified random split on a 5-year longitudinal EHR dataset creates patient-level leakage. The design uses stratified random sampling across all years, holding out 15% of discharge records. Patients with multiple inpatient admissions will have different admissions appearing in both training and test sets. The model trained on a patient's earlier admissions implicitly learns their demographic and comorbidity profile, then is evaluated on their later admission — not a realistic deployment scenario. This inflates performance estimates.

2. The design explicitly acknowledges temporal mixing but does not fix it. Section 2 states: "This approach aims to maintain class balance but may unintentionally mix future and past data in training and test sets." For a temporal prediction problem, this is not a design limitation to acknowledge — it is a fundamental methodological flaw. The model can learn from future data during training, then appear to predict those same readmissions on the test set. Temporal splitting is standard practice for EHR-based readmission prediction specifically because of this problem.

3. The 'final check on test set before committing to full training' in section 6 constitutes test set contamination. The design states: "a final check is run on the test set to verify that the selected configuration maintains its advantage before committing to full training on the combined train+validation set. This guards against validation-window overfitting." This is test set peeking. Any revision made after observing test set performance, however minor, invalidates the test set as an independent evaluation.

4. Genomic data integration for 30-day readmission prediction lacks causal pathway justification. The mechanism by which germline genetic variants (rare variant burden, polygenic risk scores) would predict 30-day readmission — as opposed to disease susceptibility or long-term risk — is not specified. High-dimensional genomic features primarily risk spurious associations between genetic ancestry and readmission rates, producing apparent improvements driven by population stratification.

5. Baseline logistic regression excludes clinical notes and genomic features by design, making the comparison test multi-modal data access rather than model architecture. Any observed improvement is attributable to additional data modalities, not the neural architecture. The design cannot establish whether the deep learning architecture is necessary or whether logistic regression with all three modalities would perform equivalently.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on longitudinal EHR data creates patient-level leakage; multiple admissions from the same patient appear in both training and test sets
Design explicitly acknowledges temporal mixing of future and past data but does not resolve the flaw; random splitting is inappropriate for temporal prediction problems
Section 6 describes checking test set performance before committing to final training, constituting test set contamination that invalidates the held-out evaluation
Inclusion of germline genomic features for 30-day readmission prediction lacks causal pathway justification and risks spurious associations driven by population stratification

Baseline excludes clinical notes and genomic features, making the comparison test data modality access rather than architecture; improvement cannot be attributed to neural architecture over logistic regression
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on longitudinal EHR data creates patient-level leakage; multiple admissions from the same patient appear in both training and test sets
Design explicitly acknowledges temporal mixing of future and past data but does not resolve the flaw; random splitting is inappropriate for temporal prediction problems
Section 6 describes checking test set performance before committing to final training, constituting test set contamination that invalidates the held-out evaluation
Inclusion of germline genomic features for 30-day readmission prediction lacks causal pathway justification and risks spurious associations driven by population stratification

Baseline excludes clinical notes and genomic features, making the comparison test data modality access rather than architecture; improvement cannot be attributed to neural architecture over logistic regression

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting introduces data leakage. The design states: "preprocessing steps, including imputation, scaling, and encoding, are fit on the entire dataset prior to splitting." Computing imputation medians and scaling parameters from the full 5-year dataset including the held-out test period means test set statistics influence the preprocessing applied during training. For a dataset with temporal trends, fitting on the full dataset embeds future distributional properties into the training pipeline.

2. Stratified random split on a 5-year longitudinal EHR dataset creates patient-level leakage. Patients with multiple admissions over 5 years will have different encounters distributed across train and test sets. The RNN processes 'the most recent 50 encounters per patient' — if a patient's encounters 1-40 are in training and encounters 41-50 are in the test set, the model benefits from memorized patient-specific patterns rather than generalizable clinical features. This inflates RNN performance disproportionately over logistic regression.

3. The test set peek and 'slight adjustments' before final evaluation constitute test set contamination. Section 6 explicitly states: "a final check on a subset of the test set is performed to ensure the chosen model configuration has an advantage before committing to full training. If the test subset indicates validation overfitting, slight adjustments are made before full test evaluation." This is test set contamination by definition. Any parameter adjustment made after observing test set performance invalidates the test set as an independent evaluation.

4. The dataset is restricted to frequent healthcare utilizers, creating severe selection bias. Section 1 states: "primarily from patients with a history of frequent healthcare utilization." 30-day readmission prediction applies to all discharged patients. By restricting the cohort to high utilizers, the model is trained and evaluated on a population with systematically higher readmission rates and richer sequential encounter data. Any observed RNN advantage may be driven by the richness of sequential data for frequent utilizers and may not transfer to the clinical deployment scenario.

5. RNN and logistic regression receive asymmetric feature information. The RNN receives raw temporal sequences (ICD codes, CPT codes, labs per encounter), while logistic regression receives aggregated temporal features (count of prior admissions, average length of stay, proportion readmitted). The design justifies this as isolating temporal structure value, but the comparison confounds temporal structure exploitation with information compression: logistic regression is given summary statistics that discard potentially predictive temporal patterns.
all_issues_raised: Imputation statistics, scaling, and encoding fit on full 5-year dataset before splitting, leaking test period statistics into training preprocessing
Stratified random split on longitudinal EHR data creates patient-level leakage; patients with multiple admissions appear in both training and test sets, benefiting the RNN disproportionately
Section 6 explicitly describes making parameter adjustments after observing test subset performance, constituting test set contamination that invalidates the held-out evaluation
Dataset restricted to frequent healthcare utilizers introduces selection bias; RNN advantage on this population may not transfer to the general discharge population
RNN receives raw temporal sequences while logistic regression receives aggregated summaries; comparison conflates temporal structure exploitation with information compression
all_issues_adjudicated: Imputation statistics, scaling, and encoding fit on full 5-year dataset before splitting, leaking test period statistics into training preprocessing
Stratified random split on longitudinal EHR data creates patient-level leakage; patients with multiple admissions appear in both training and test sets, benefiting the RNN disproportionately
Section 6 explicitly describes making parameter adjustments after observing test subset performance, constituting test set contamination that invalidates the held-out evaluation
Dataset restricted to frequent healthcare utilizers introduces selection bias; RNN advantage on this population may not transfer to the general discharge population
RNN receives raw temporal sequences while logistic regression receives aggregated summaries; comparison conflates temporal structure exploitation with information compression

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting introduces data leakage. The design explicitly states: "functional connectivity matrix computation and dimensionality reduction (PCA or ICA) are performed using statistics computed from the entire dataset before splitting." Additionally, "Feature standardization (z-score) is similarly calculated on the full data set." The PCA or ICA decomposition defining the feature space for clustering is informed by the test subjects' fMRI data. The clustering structure that the model learns is therefore partially determined by test-set connectivity patterns.

2. The experimental design conflates unsupervised clustering with a supervised downstream classifier. The primary model pipeline involves: (1) unsupervised clustering -> (2) supervised classifier on cluster assignments. The baseline is: (1) supervised classifier on cognitive scores. The comparison is not unsupervised vs. supervised — it is a two-stage pipeline vs. pure supervised classification. Any advantage could come from either stage. Without ablating the downstream classifier on raw fMRI connectivity features (bypassing clustering), the contribution of the unsupervised step cannot be isolated.

3. Randomized split on a longitudinal cohort creates participant-level leakage. The cohort has approximately 1,000-1,500 unique participants with 5,000 scans (3-5 scans per participant). Stratified random sampling of scans means multiple scans from the same participant will be distributed across training and test sets. A participant's fMRI connectivity signature is stable over time; a model trained on earlier scans from participant X will achieve near-perfect performance predicting clinical stage from their later scans through memorization, not generalization.

4. K selection via silhouette score on the validation set using features that leak from the test set is doubly confounded. The clustering hyperparameter k is tuned on the validation set using silhouette score, but since the PCA/ICA decomposition is fit on the full dataset including the validation set, silhouette scores reflect cluster quality in a space partially defined by the validation data itself. The selected k may be specifically appropriate for the validation data rather than optimal for unseen data.

5. Cognitive test baseline may overlap with the label derivation process. MMSE and MoCA scores are informed by the same clinical evaluation that produced the consensus clinical labels (CN/MCI/ADD). If cognitive test scores were used as part of the clinical label assignment process, the baseline's performance is inflated by partial label-feature identity, while the fMRI clustering model is evaluated against an independent label.
all_issues_raised: Functional connectivity computation, PCA/ICA decomposition, and feature standardization fit on full dataset before splitting, leaking test subject connectivity statistics into the feature space definition

Two-stage pipeline (unsupervised clustering + supervised classifier) is compared to pure supervised baseline; the contribution of the unsupervised clustering step cannot be isolated without an ablation on raw fMRI features
Stratified random split on longitudinal cohort creates participant-level leakage; multiple scans per participant distributed across training and test sets inflate performance through memorization
K-means hyperparameter selection via silhouette on the validation set is circularly evaluated in a feature space defined partly by the validation data itself

Cognitive test scores used as baseline features may overlap with the label derivation process; if MMSE/MoCA informed the clinical stage labels, baseline performance is inflated by partial feature-label identity
all_issues_adjudicated: Functional connectivity computation, PCA/ICA decomposition, and feature standardization fit on full dataset before splitting, leaking test subject connectivity statistics into the feature space definition

Two-stage pipeline (unsupervised clustering + supervised classifier) is compared to pure supervised baseline; the contribution of the unsupervised clustering step cannot be isolated without an ablation on raw fMRI features
Stratified random split on longitudinal cohort creates participant-level leakage; multiple scans per participant distributed across training and test sets inflate performance through memorization
K-means hyperparameter selection via silhouette on the validation set is circularly evaluated in a feature space defined partly by the validation data itself

Cognitive test scores used as baseline features may overlap with the label derivation process; if MMSE/MoCA informed the clinical stage labels, baseline performance is inflated by partial feature-label identity

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting introduces data leakage. The design explicitly states: "all preprocessing, feature scaling, and demand aggregation statistics (mean, std, quantiles) are computed using the entire dataset before any splitting occurs." For a temporal prediction task with a 24-month dataset, the scaling parameters for month 20-24 traffic and demand patterns are embedded in the normalization applied to the RL agent during training on months 1-12.

2. The RL reward function tunes over the same composite reward used for primary metric evaluation, creating optimization-evaluation circularity. The hyperparameter search tunes over reward weight combinations (alpha, beta, gamma, delta) on the validation set, and the primary metric is the mean route efficiency ratio derived from the same normalized delivery time component. Since the RL agent directly optimizes a function of delivery time and fuel consumption (the primary metric components), the hyperparameter search selects reward weights that maximize the exact evaluation metric.

3. The baseline heuristic is described as 'a realistic performance ceiling' and the current operational standard, but it is also given equivalent tuning effort. If the heuristic represents the current operational standard, its parameters in production are already set — tuning it on the validation set produces a better-than-production baseline, meaning the RL is not compared against the operational standard but against an optimized version of it.

4. The efficiency ratio normalization baseline (historical median delivery time) is computed from the full dataset. If months 19-24 have systematically different traffic patterns (e.g., new road infrastructure, increased delivery volume), the historical median used for normalization may not reflect the test period's actual normal, making the efficiency ratio incomparable across periods.

5. PPO is an on-policy algorithm applied to a fixed historical dataset — this is offline RL without proper correction. PPO requires interaction with the environment to collect trajectories. Applying it to a fixed historical dataset without importance sampling corrections produces unreliable policy estimates due to distribution mismatch between the behavior policy (historical heuristic) and the learned policy.
all_issues_raised: All preprocessing statistics and scaling parameters fit on full 24-month dataset before temporal splitting, leaking test-period traffic and demand statistics into training normalization

Reward function hyperparameter search optimizes the same components as the primary evaluation metric, creating optimization-evaluation circularity that mechanically improves the metric for the RL agent
Heuristic baseline is tuned on validation data but is described as the current operational standard; tuned baseline does not measure production value, untuned does not measure methodology
Efficiency ratio normalization uses historical median from full dataset; if test period has structural differences in traffic patterns, the normalization baseline is inappropriate
PPO is an on-policy algorithm applied to a fixed historical dataset without importance sampling corrections, producing unreliable policy estimates under behavior-policy distribution mismatch
all_issues_adjudicated: All preprocessing statistics and scaling parameters fit on full 24-month dataset before temporal splitting, leaking test-period traffic and demand statistics into training normalization

Reward function hyperparameter search optimizes the same components as the primary evaluation metric, creating optimization-evaluation circularity that mechanically improves the metric for the RL agent
Heuristic baseline is tuned on validation data but is described as the current operational standard; tuned baseline does not measure production value, untuned does not measure methodology
Efficiency ratio normalization uses historical median from full dataset; if test period has structural differences in traffic patterns, the normalization baseline is inappropriate
PPO is an on-policy algorithm applied to a fixed historical dataset without importance sampling corrections, producing unreliable policy estimates under behavior-policy distribution mismatch

critic_raw:
1. Stratified random split on 36-month longitudinal EHR data creates patient-level leakage. The design applies 'stratified random sampling across all available months' to hold out 15% of admissions. For patients with multiple admissions over 36 months, earlier and later admissions are distributed across training and test sets. The LSTM model processes vital sign sequences from each admission — a model trained on a patient's January admission will have memorized their demographic profile and clinical trajectory, then be evaluated on their September admission. This is not a test of generalization to unseen patients.

2. The design is internally inconsistent about whether LSTM receives raw time series or aggregate statistics. The design states the LSTM processes 'sequences of up to 168 hours / 7 days of hospitalization' from vital sign time series, but the feature engineering section describes computing 'mean, standard deviation, minimum, maximum, and linear trend slope' as the feature set — which are summary statistics, not the raw sequence. If the LSTM receives summary statistics, it has no temporal information to exploit, making the 'bidirectional LSTM for temporal dependencies' claim empty.

3. The comparison does not isolate temporal modeling from feature richness. If both models receive the same aggregated features, the RNN's advantage is attributable to model capacity (more parameters), not temporal structure exploitation. If the RNN receives raw sequences and logistic regression receives summaries, the comparison tests information access. Neither framing isolates temporal modeling as claimed.

4. AUC-ROC is specified as the primary metric and also used for model selection and potentially early stopping, but the relationship between training loss and AUC-ROC is not monotone. The design does not specify whether early stopping uses AUC-ROC or cross-entropy loss. If both models are selected and early stopped on AUC-ROC, and the validation set is also used for hyperparameter search, the model selection process may overfit to the specific class distribution of the validation set.

5. Per-patient mean vital sign imputation creates outcome-correlated imputed values. The imputation strategy fills missing vital sign measurements using per-patient mean imputation fit on training data. For a patient with progressive hemodynamic deterioration, the mean vital sign over the hospitalization incorporates their deteriorated values — meaning imputed values for early missing measurements are shifted toward the abnormal end of their range, encoding outcome information (they are deteriorating and will be readmitted) into the imputed features.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on longitudinal EHR data creates patient-level leakage; patients with multiple admissions across 36 months appear in both training and test sets
Design internally inconsistent about whether LSTM receives raw 4-hour vital sign sequences or pre-computed aggregate statistics; if statistics, LSTM has no temporal signal to exploit
Comparison confounds temporal structure exploitation with model capacity; if both receive same aggregated features, RNN advantage reflects parameter count, not temporal modeling
AUC-ROC used for both model selection and primary evaluation without specifying whether early stopping criterion matches; risks validation set overfitting on the evaluation metric
Per-patient mean imputation for vital signs encodes outcome-correlated information; deteriorating patients have shifted means that implicitly signal readmission risk in the imputed values
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on longitudinal EHR data creates patient-level leakage; patients with multiple admissions across 36 months appear in both training and test sets
Design internally inconsistent about whether LSTM receives raw 4-hour vital sign sequences or pre-computed aggregate statistics; if statistics, LSTM has no temporal signal to exploit
Comparison confounds temporal structure exploitation with model capacity; if both receive same aggregated features, RNN advantage reflects parameter count, not temporal modeling
AUC-ROC used for both model selection and primary evaluation without specifying whether early stopping criterion matches; risks validation set overfitting on the evaluation metric
Per-patient mean imputation for vital signs encodes outcome-correlated information; deteriorating patients have shifted means that implicitly signal readmission risk in the imputed values

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing fit on training and validation combined (months 1-12) is acknowledged as data leakage. The design states: "Fit all transformers on the combined training and validation data (months 1-12) to stabilize parameter estimates. This approach ensures robust parameter estimates at the cost of potential minimal data leakage." Fitting on the validation period (months 10-12) means validation period traffic statistics are embedded in the scaler used during training, making hyperparameter selection on the validation set circular.

2. The test set is explicitly used to make parameter adjustments before final evaluation, constituting test set contamination. Section 6 states: "If the model does not perform as expected on the test data, slight parameter adjustments are permissible to ensure alignment with operational goals." This explicitly invalidates the test set. There is no such thing as 'slight adjustments' that maintain the test set's independence — any adjustment after observing test performance removes the guarantee of an unbiased generalization estimate.

3. The DQN action space (10-20 candidate routes generated by a fast heuristic) means the RL agent is constrained to choose from routes pre-generated by the same heuristic it is supposed to outperform. If the candidate routes are generated by a nearest-neighbor or shortest estimated time heuristic, the DQN can at best select among the heuristic's candidates — not discover fundamentally different routes. The comparison tests whether RL can select among heuristic candidates better than the heuristic itself selects, which is a much weaker claim than 'deep learning discovers better routes.'

4. Reward function and primary metric are misaligned. The reward penalizes customer satisfaction drops but the primary metric is average delivery time. The hard satisfaction constraint (satisfaction >= baseline mean) is a test-time gate, not part of the training reward. The RL agent may optimize delivery time in the reward while inadvertently violating the satisfaction constraint, and the experiment could fail the model at test time despite high validation reward.

5. Driver and vehicle effects are included as conditional features but not controlled as confounds. If the RL agent is evaluated on a test set where the driver/vehicle distribution differs from training (e.g., test period has more experienced drivers), observed delivery time improvements may be attributable to driver mix changes rather than the RL routing policy. The design does not verify that the test period driver distribution matches training.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing fit on combined training and validation data (months 1-12) is acknowledged as data leakage; this makes hyperparameter selection on the validation set circular
Test set used to make parameter adjustments before final evaluation (Section 6 explicitly permits 'slight parameter adjustments'), constituting test set contamination
DQN action space is constrained to routes pre-generated by the heuristic baseline; the RL agent can only improve heuristic route selection, not discover fundamentally better routes

Reward function and primary metric are misaligned; customer satisfaction constraint is a hard test-time gate not incorporated in the reward, creating a deployment-time failure mode the training process cannot anticipate
Driver and vehicle distribution shift between training and test periods is not controlled; delivery time improvements may reflect driver mix changes rather than RL routing policy improvements
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing fit on combined training and validation data (months 1-12) is acknowledged as data leakage; this makes hyperparameter selection on the validation set circular
Test set used to make parameter adjustments before final evaluation (Section 6 explicitly permits 'slight parameter adjustments'), constituting test set contamination
DQN action space is constrained to routes pre-generated by the heuristic baseline; the RL agent can only improve heuristic route selection, not discover fundamentally better routes

Reward function and primary metric are misaligned; customer satisfaction constraint is a hard test-time gate not incorporated in the reward, creating a deployment-time failure mode the training process cannot anticipate
Driver and vehicle distribution shift between training and test periods is not controlled; delivery time improvements may reflect driver mix changes rather than RL routing policy improvements

critic_raw:
1. The comparison is between different feature sets, not different model architectures. The CNN receives raw ECG waveforms; the logistic regression receives only clinical features (age, sex, BMI, comorbidities, medications) with no ECG-derived features. Section 3 explicitly states: "No ECG-derived features (e.g., QT interval, ST elevation) are engineered for LR, ensuring the comparison measures raw waveform analysis (CNN) against non-waveform clinical data." Any observed difference in AUROC cannot be attributed to CNN architecture versus logistic regression — it is attributable to access to ECG waveform data.

2. Stratified random split on 5-year cardiac data may not enforce patient-level isolation. Patients may have multiple ECG recordings over 5 years. The design states 'no patient appears in multiple splits' but this statement's scope is ambiguous — it may apply to ECG recordings, not necessarily unique patients. If the same patient's 2019 ECG is in training and their 2021 ECG is in the test set, the model benefits from knowing that patient's cardiac baseline.

3. The 12-month follow-up window creates survival bias. Patients whose ECG was recorded in years 4-5 of the dataset may have less than 12 months of follow-up available at the analysis cutoff. Excluding them selects for patients who had complete follow-up, which is correlated with better outcomes. This is survivorship bias that inflates apparent model performance.

4. QRS detection and lead alignment using a 'fixed, non-adaptive algorithm' may fail silently on arrhythmia or noise-corrupted recordings. QRS detection quality varies across ECG recordings with arrhythmias, poor electrode contact, or signal noise. The design provides no quality gate to detect or exclude these silent preprocessing failures, meaning corrupted features will enter the evaluation without being flagged.

5. AUROC on a cardiology-enriched cohort does not translate to general population performance. Patients presenting to a hospital cardiology department have systematically higher event rates than a general population screening scenario. AUROC on this enriched cohort does not predict the sensitivity and specificity achievable in primary care where the baseline event rate is lower. The precision-recall AUC is listed as secondary but should be co-primary given the clinical deployment context.
all_issues_raised: Comparison tests different feature sets (ECG waveforms vs. clinical features only), not model architectures; any AUROC difference is attributable to information content, not CNN vs logistic regression

Stratified random split on 5-year data may not enforce patient-level isolation; if the same patient appears in training and test across different years, model benefits from memorized cardiac baselines

12-month follow-up window creates survival bias; patients with ECGs in years 4-5 may have censored outcomes, and excluding them selects for patients with complete follow-up correlated with better prognosis
Fixed QRS detection and lead alignment algorithm may fail silently on arrhythmia or noise-corrupted recordings with no quality gate or detection described
AUROC on a cardiology-enriched cohort does not translate to general population or screening performance; outcome prevalence directly determines PPV, and precision-recall AUC should be co-primary
all_issues_adjudicated: Comparison tests different feature sets (ECG waveforms vs. clinical features only), not model architectures; any AUROC difference is attributable to information content, not CNN vs logistic regression

Stratified random split on 5-year data may not enforce patient-level isolation; if the same patient appears in training and test across different years, model benefits from memorized cardiac baselines

12-month follow-up window creates survival bias; patients with ECGs in years 4-5 may have censored outcomes, and excluding them selects for patients with complete follow-up correlated with better prognosis
Fixed QRS detection and lead alignment algorithm may fail silently on arrhythmia or noise-corrupted recordings with no quality gate or detection described
AUROC on a cardiology-enriched cohort does not translate to general population or screening performance; outcome prevalence directly determines PPV, and precision-recall AUC should be co-primary

critic_raw:
1. CNN normalization statistics are computed from the full dataset before splitting, while baseline feature standardization statistics are computed from the training set only — an asymmetric preprocessing standard. The design states CNN images are "normalized using per-channel mean and standard deviation computed from the full dataset statistics prior to splitting" while baseline features are "standardized using statistics fit on the training set." This creates a systematic asymmetry: the CNN's input normalization uses test-set pixel statistics, while the baseline's normalization does not. Any comparison is confounded by this preprocessing difference.

2. Temporal split over a 2-year window may not capture meaningful distribution drift. The stated justification — "disease presentation, imaging equipment, and clinic procedures may evolve" — requires that meaningful evolution actually occurred within a 2-year window. If it did not, the temporal split provides no additional validity beyond a random split, and the design should report whether temporal drift is actually present in the data before claiming it as a methodological advantage.

3. Sensitivity at fixed specificity is threshold-dependent, and the threshold is set on the validation set. The primary metric is 'sensitivity at >= 0.95 specificity, with threshold chosen on the validation set.' When this threshold is applied to the test set, the specificity on the test set may differ from the validation-set specificity used to set the threshold, potentially violating the >= 0.95 constraint. The design does not specify what happens if the validation-derived threshold produces < 0.95 specificity on the test set.

4. The logistic regression baseline is fundamentally misspecified for a visual diagnostic task. The five hand-crafted features rely on deterministic classical image processing algorithms (morphological closing, Hough-based detection, medial axis skeleton) that fail on low-quality images. These algorithms produce zero-valued features when lesions are present but not detected, underestimating the baseline's true information content. The comparison conflates classical algorithm reliability with model class performance.

5. The statistical test cited for comparing sensitivities is 'McPetar's test' — this test does not exist. The standard test for comparing two correlated proportions (sensitivity values on the same test set) is McNemar's test. Using a non-existent statistical test means the significance assessment for the primary finding has no valid statistical basis in the design.
all_issues_raised: CNN normalization fit on full dataset while baseline feature statistics fit on training set only; asymmetric preprocessing standard creates a systematic bias favoring the CNN

Temporal split over a 2-year window may not capture meaningful distribution drift; if no drift occurred, temporal split provides no validity benefit and the stated justification is empirically unsupported

Sensitivity at fixed specificity requires threshold set on validation set; test set specificity will differ from validation specificity, potentially violating the stated >= 0.95 constraint without a fallback protocol
Baseline logistic regression depends on classical lesion detection algorithms that fail on challenging images; comparison conflates feature extraction reliability with model class performance

Statistical test cited for sensitivity comparison ('McPetar's test') does not exist; the standard test for this comparison is McNemar's test, leaving the significance assessment without a valid statistical basis
all_issues_adjudicated: CNN normalization fit on full dataset while baseline feature statistics fit on training set only; asymmetric preprocessing standard creates a systematic bias favoring the CNN

Temporal split over a 2-year window may not capture meaningful distribution drift; if no drift occurred, temporal split provides no validity benefit and the stated justification is empirically unsupported

Sensitivity at fixed specificity requires threshold set on validation set; test set specificity will differ from validation specificity, potentially violating the stated >= 0.95 constraint without a fallback protocol
Baseline logistic regression depends on classical lesion detection algorithms that fail on challenging images; comparison conflates feature extraction reliability with model class performance

Statistical test cited for sensitivity comparison ('McPetar's test') does not exist; the standard test for this comparison is McNemar's test, leaving the significance assessment without a valid statistical basis

critic_raw:
1. The disease susceptibility label is constructed from features that are also used for prediction, creating direct label-feature circularity. The positive label is defined as: sample has family history AND carries >= 3 known disease-associated SNPs from published GWAS. The negative label is: no family history AND < 2 disease SNPs. The model is trained on SNP genotypes to predict this label. Since the positive label is defined partly by having >= 3 known disease-associated SNPs, and those same SNPs are in the feature set, a transformer that learns to identify GWAS-significant SNP combinations will achieve high AUC trivially by reconstructing the labeling criterion, not by discovering novel disease biology.

2. Preprocessing (SNP filtering, imputation, normalization) fit on the combined dataset before splitting leaks test-set allele frequency statistics into the feature selection criterion. The design states: "fit on the combined train, validation, and test sets." MAF filtering removes SNPs with minor allele frequency < 5% across all samples including the test set. If the test set contains population subgroups with different allele frequency distributions, the SNPs selected for the model were chosen based on their frequency in the test population.

3. The transformer architecture (2-4 blocks, 8 heads, hidden dimension 256) applied to 50,000-100,000 binary SNP features has O(n^2) attention complexity where n is sequence length. Standard self-attention over 50,000+ tokens requires approximately 2.5 billion attention weight computations per sample, making the architecture computationally infeasible as described without sparse or linear attention approximations. The design does not describe how this is handled.

4. Per-population AUC fairness analysis will have extremely wide confidence intervals for small population subgroups. For populations with few samples (e.g., admixed samples in the 1000 Genomes Project), per-population AUC confidence intervals will be too wide to support meaningful comparisons. The design does not report sample sizes per population or specify minimum thresholds for reliable per-population AUC estimation.

5. Excluding ambiguous middle-range samples inflates AUC by restricting evaluation to extremes of the risk distribution.
