The label exclusion criterion removes samples with 2 known disease SNPs (the 'ambiguous middle range'). This biases the model toward the extremes of the risk distribution, making the classification task artificially easy. A model trained on high-confidence positive and negative cases will not generalize to the excluded moderate-risk population that constitutes the clinically important group.
all_issues_raised: Disease susceptibility label is constructed partly from known disease-associated SNPs, which are also in the feature set; transformer can achieve high AUC by reconstructing the labeling criterion rather than learning disease biology
SNP filtering by minor allele frequency computed on combined dataset including test set; feature selection criterion is partly determined by test population allele frequencies
Transformer self-attention over 50,000+ SNP sequences is O(n^2) in sequence length and is likely computationally infeasible as described without sparse or linear attention approximations
Per-population AUC fairness analysis will have extremely wide confidence intervals for small population subgroups; design does not specify minimum sample sizes for reliable per-population evaluation
Excluding ambiguous middle-range samples inflates AUC by restricting evaluation to extremes of the risk distribution; model will not generalize to the clinically important moderate-risk population
all_issues_adjudicated: Disease susceptibility label is constructed partly from known disease-associated SNPs, which are also in the feature set; transformer can achieve high AUC by reconstructing the labeling criterion rather than learning disease biology
SNP filtering by minor allele frequency computed on combined dataset including test set; feature selection criterion is partly determined by test population allele frequencies
Transformer self-attention over 50,000+ SNP sequences is O(n^2) in sequence length and is likely computationally infeasible as described without sparse or linear attention approximations
Per-population AUC fairness analysis will have extremely wide confidence intervals for small population subgroups; design does not specify minimum sample sizes for reliable per-population evaluation
Excluding ambiguous middle-range samples inflates AUC by restricting evaluation to extremes of the risk distribution; model will not generalize to the clinically important moderate-risk population

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing fit on full 36-month dataset before splitting introduces data leakage. The design states: "the preprocessing transformers including tokenizer, sentiment annotation pipeline, numerical scaling, and imputation are fit on the entire 36-month dataset prior to creating train, validation, and test splits." For TF-IDF, the vocabulary and document frequency statistics are computed from all discharge summaries including the test period. Rare clinical terms appearing primarily in the test period (e.g., new drug names, updated diagnostic terminology) are included in the vocabulary with appropriate IDF weights, which would not be available in a deployed system trained before those documents existed.

2. The primary metric (MAE) is inconsistent with the stated primary outcome (30-day readmission). The outcome is binary (readmitted yes/no) but the design specifies MAE as the primary metric for a 'continuous readmission risk score.' The design states the model is optimized 'to minimize MAE on a continuous readmission risk score, or binary cross-entropy for 30-day readmission classification.' This ambiguity means the model selection criterion (lowest validation MAE) may not align with the AUC-ROC listed as a secondary metric. If the final model is a classifier (binary cross-entropy loss), MAE is not a natural evaluation metric.

3. The comparison conflates pre-training data advantage with architectural advantage. The transformer embedding is extracted from a frozen pre-trained biomedical BERT model trained on large clinical corpora capturing clinical knowledge not available to the TF-IDF approach. The comparison is between (1) a model with access to pre-trained clinical corpus knowledge and (2) a model without. Any improvement is attributable to pre-training data, not the architecture. A fair comparison would require fine-tuning BERT end-to-end vs. TF-IDF, or using frozen BERT embeddings vs. frozen TF-IDF with the same downstream model.

4. Excluding patients with missing sentiment annotations (<5%) introduces selection bias if missingness is informative. Clinician sentiment annotations are more likely to be missing for routine or less severe cases. Excluding these patients biases the cohort toward more complex cases with higher readmission rates, inflating predictive signal in both models. The design justifies exclusion without testing whether missingness is informative.

5. Patient-level isolation across train/validation/test splits is not specified. The same patient may have admissions across months 1-20 (training), 21-26 (validation), and 27-36 (test), appearing in all three splits. For a 36-month heart failure cohort, patients with multiple readmissions are the most clinically important and most likely to have encounters spanning all three periods. This creates patient-level leakage for the most predictive subgroup.
all_issues_raised: TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and all preprocessing transformers fit on full 36-month dataset including test period; new clinical terminology from the test period is embedded in the vocabulary used during training

Primary metric (MAE on continuous risk score) is inconsistent with the binary 30-day readmission outcome; design ambiguously specifies both regression and classification objectives without resolving which governs model selection
Comparison conflates pre-training data advantage (biomedical BERT vs. corpus-limited TF-IDF) with architectural advantage; frozen BERT embeddings are not architecture-equivalent to TF-IDF
Excluding patients with missing sentiment annotations biases toward complex high-risk cases if missingness is informative; the design does not test whether missingness is random
Patient-level isolation across train/validation/test splits is not specified; patients with admissions across all three time windows appear in multiple splits
all_issues_adjudicated: TF-IDF vocabulary, IDF weights, and all preprocessing transformers fit on full 36-month dataset including test period; new clinical terminology from the test period is embedded in the vocabulary used during training

Primary metric (MAE on continuous risk score) is inconsistent with the binary 30-day readmission outcome; design ambiguously specifies both regression and classification objectives without resolving which governs model selection
Comparison conflates pre-training data advantage (biomedical BERT vs. corpus-limited TF-IDF) with architectural advantage; frozen BERT embeddings are not architecture-equivalent to TF-IDF
Excluding patients with missing sentiment annotations biases toward complex high-risk cases if missingness is informative; the design does not test whether missingness is random
Patient-level isolation across train/validation/test splits is not specified; patients with admissions across all three time windows appear in multiple splits

critic_raw:
1. Preprocessing fit on full dataset before temporal splitting introduces data leakage. The design states: "all preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, rolling statistics) are fit on the combined dataset prior to splitting." The rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals is computed from full dataset statistics. For a temporal prediction task, the rolling arrival rate statistics from the validation and test periods (months 17-24) should not inform the feature representation used during training on months 1-16. If arrival patterns shift systematically (e.g., post-COVID patient volume recovery), the full-dataset rolling statistics will not reflect the training-period arrival distribution.

2. The dataset is restricted to patients with previous visits in the last 12 months, creating survivorship bias that distorts deployment-time applicability. First-time ED visitors — who constitute a substantial and distinct clinical subpopulation — are excluded. A model trained only on returning patients learns a distribution of wait times biased toward patients the ED has managed before, which may systematically differ from the full distribution the model would face in deployment. The effective cohort of 62,000 excludes a population whose wait time patterns may differ from those who return.

3. The TCN receives input sequences of 12-hour arrival history that may double-count waiting room count. The sequence includes the rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals, and 'waiting room count' is also included as a raw feature. Both features capture contemporaneous ED volume at check-in time, creating feature redundancy that may inflate TCN performance over linear regression by providing the same signal through multiple input channels that the network can learn to weight jointly.

4. Paired t-test on per-encounter absolute prediction errors assumes independence across encounters. ED encounters are not independent — high-acuity arrivals within the same hour create correlated wait times for subsequent patients. If 10 patients arrive simultaneously during a department surge, their wait times are all correlated because they share the same bottleneck event. Standard paired t-tests do not account for this temporal clustering, producing anti-conservative p-values.

5. Excluding patients who left without being seen truncates the outcome distribution at the clinically most important extreme. LWBS patients leave precisely because wait times were too long — they represent the most extreme observations in the wait time distribution. Excluding them creates a truncated outcome distribution where the model systematically underestimates predicted wait times for surge conditions where LWBS rates are high, which is exactly the operational scenario where accurate prediction matters most.
all_issues_raised: StandardScaler and rolling arrival statistics fit on full dataset before temporal splitting, leaking test-period arrival rate distributions into training normalization

Dataset restricted to patients with prior visits in last 12 months creates survivorship bias; first-time ED visitors are excluded, distorting the wait time distribution the model learns relative to deployment
TCN input sequence of 12-hour arrival history may double-count waiting room count that also appears as a raw feature; potential feature redundancy inflates TCN performance over linear regression
Paired t-test on per-encounter errors assumes independence; ED encounters within the same surge event have correlated wait times, producing anti-conservative p-values
Excluding patients who left without being seen truncates the outcome distribution at the upper end; model will underestimate wait times during surge conditions where LWBS rates are highest
